title: Waves and Sound
subtitle: How sound is made and travels, pitch and loudness, hearing, and the speed of sound.

studyGuide.heading: How sound is made
studyGuide.html:
• Sound is made by vibrations. A vibrating object (a guitar string, a drum skin, vocal cords) makes the air particles next to it vibrate.
  • These vibrations are passed on from particle to particle as a sound wave.
  • Sound is a longitudinal wave — the particles vibrate back and forth in the same direction the wave travels, making areas squashed together (compressions) and spread out (rarefactions).

studyGuide.heading: Sound needs a medium
studyGuide.html:
• Sound needs particles to travel through (a solid, liquid or gas), so it cannot travel through a vacuum (empty space).
  • Sound travels fastest in solids (particles close together pass the vibration quickly), slower in liquids, and slowest in gases.
  • In space there is no air, so no sound — this is why 'in space, no one can hear you'.
studyGuide.mnemonic: Sound speed: Solids > Liquids > Gases. Closer particles = faster sound.

studyGuide.heading: Frequency and pitch; amplitude and loudness
studyGuide.html:
• Frequency = number of vibrations per second, measured in hertz (Hz). Higher frequency → higher pitch.
  • Amplitude = the size of the vibration. Bigger amplitude → louder sound.

diagram.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.a{stroke:#1f2a44;stroke-width:1}.w{fill:none;stroke:#5b8def;stroke-width:2.5}.t{font:10px Nunito;fill:#1f2a44}</style><line class='a' x1='10' y1='40' x2='170' y2='40'/><path class='w' d='M10 40 Q30 10 50 40 T90 40 T130 40 T170 40'/><text class='t' x='40' y='70' text-anchor='middle'>low frequency</text><text class='t' x='40' y='84' text-anchor='middle'>(low pitch)</text><line class='a' x1='190' y1='40' x2='350' y2='40'/><path class='w' d='M190 40 Q200 18 210 40 T230 40 T250 40 T270 40 T290 40 T310 40 T330 40 T350 40'/><text class='t' x='270' y='70' text-anchor='middle'>high frequency</text><text class='t' x='270' y='84' text-anchor='middle'>(high pitch)</text><line class='a' x1='10' y1='118' x2='170' y2='118'/><path class='w' d='M10 118 Q40 95 70 118 T130 118 T170 118' stroke='#2b9d6b'/><text class='t' x='90' y='135' text-anchor='middle'>small amplitude = quiet</text></svg>
diagram.caption: More waves squeezed in = higher pitch. Taller waves = louder.

studyGuide.heading: The oscilloscope (CRO)
studyGuide.html:
An oscilloscope turns a sound into a wave you can see on a screen.

  • A higher-pitched sound shows waves that are closer together (higher frequency).
  • A louder sound shows taller waves (bigger amplitude).

studyGuide.heading: Hearing and the ear
studyGuide.html:
• Sound waves enter the ear and make the eardrum vibrate.
  • Tiny bones pass the vibrations to the cochlea, which turns them into nerve signals sent to the brain.
  • Humans hear roughly 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz. Sounds above this are ultrasound; we lose the ability to hear high frequencies as we age.
  • Very loud sounds can damage the ear — wear ear protection.

diagram.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 380 175' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.s{stroke:#1f2a44;stroke-width:2;fill:none}.t{font:10px Nunito;fill:#1f2a44}.lab{font:bold 10px Nunito;fill:#3f6fd1}</style><path d='M28 55 q-18 45 12 88 q34 -4 38 -44 q-6 -50 -50 -44 z' fill='#fde6ef' stroke='#e0524d' stroke-width='2'/><text class='t' x='8' y='160'>pinna (outer ear)</text><text class='lab' x='6' y='42'>sound in →</text><rect x='70' y='78' width='62' height='26' fill='#eaf1fe' stroke='#1f2a44' stroke-width='2'/><text class='t' x='74' y='124'>ear canal</text><line x1='134' y1='70' x2='134' y2='112' stroke='#e0524d' stroke-width='4'/><text class='lab' x='104' y='60'>eardrum</text><circle cx='150' cy='86' r='5' fill='#f0a35a' stroke='#1f2a44'/><circle cx='164' cy='97' r='5' fill='#f0a35a' stroke='#1f2a44'/><circle cx='178' cy='86' r='5' fill='#f0a35a' stroke='#1f2a44'/><text class='t' x='150' y='74'>tiny bones</text><path d='M250 96 a22 22 0 1 1 -16 -21 a14 14 0 1 1 10 14 a7 7 0 1 1 -5 -7' fill='none' stroke='#5b8def' stroke-width='3'/><text class='lab' x='222' y='150'>cochlea</text><line x1='190' y1='90' x2='214' y2='92' class='s'/><line x1='276' y1='92' x2='345' y2='92' stroke='#2b9d6b' stroke-width='2.5'/><text class='lab' x='300' y='84'>→ brain</text></svg>
diagram.caption: Sound funnels in, vibrates the eardrum, the bones pass it to the cochlea, which sends signals to the brain.

studyGuide.mnemonic: The sound's journey through the ear: Drum → Bones → Cochlea → Brain.

studyGuide.heading: Echoes and the speed of sound
studyGuide.html:
• An echo is sound reflected off a hard surface.
  • The speed of sound in air is about 330–340 m/s — much slower than light (300,000,000 m/s).
  • That is why you see lightning before you hear thunder, and see a distant hammer hit before you hear it.
  • Speed = distance ÷ time can be used with echoes to work out distances.

flashcards.back: A vibrating object, which makes the air particles vibrate.

flashcards.front: What type of wave is sound?
flashcards.back: A longitudinal wave (particles vibrate in the same direction the wave travels).

flashcards.front: Can sound travel through a vacuum?
flashcards.back: No — sound needs particles (a medium) to travel through.

flashcards.front: In which state does sound travel fastest?
What is frequency and its unit?
flashcards.back: The number of vibrations per second, measured in hertz (Hz).

flashcards.front: On an oscilloscope, a high pitch looks like…
On an oscilloscope, a loud sound looks like…

flashcards.back: Vibrates when sound waves hit it, passing vibrations into the ear.
Turns vibrations into nerve signals sent to the brain.
Sound with a frequency above 20,000 Hz (too high for humans to hear).
Sound reflected off a hard surface.

commonMistakes.title: Saying sound can travel through space
commonMistakes.detail: Space is a vacuum with no particles, so sound cannot travel — only light can.

commonMistakes.title: Mixing up frequency and amplitude
commonMistakes.detail: Frequency controls pitch (high/low); amplitude controls loudness (loud/quiet).

commonMistakes.title: Saying sound travels fastest in gases
commonMistakes.detail: Sound is fastest in solids because the particles are closest and pass on vibrations quickly.

commonMistakes.title: Calling sound a transverse wave
commonMistakes.detail: Sound is longitudinal — particles vibrate along the direction the wave travels.

commonMistakes.title: Confusing loud with high-pitched
commonMistakes.detail: A loud sound can be low-pitched; loudness (amplitude) and pitch (frequency) are different.

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting light is much faster than sound
commonMistakes.detail: You see lightning before hearing thunder because light travels almost instantly compared to sound.

questions.q: What is needed to make a sound?
questions.explanation: All sounds are made by something vibrating.
questions.hint: Think of a guitar string or drum.

questions.q: Sound cannot travel through…
questions.explanation: A vacuum has no particles, so sound cannot travel through it.
questions.hint: What's missing in empty space?

questions.q: Sound travels fastest in…
questions.explanation: Particles are closest in a solid, so vibrations pass on fastest.
questions.hint: Where are particles closest together?

questions.q: Higher frequency means a…
questions.hint: Frequency links to pitch.

questions.q: A bigger amplitude makes a sound…
questions.hint: Amplitude links to loudness.

questions.q: The unit of frequency is the…
questions.hint: Hz.

questions.q: Explain why there is no sound in space.
questions.modelAnswer: Space is a vacuum, which means there are no particles. Sound needs particles to pass the vibrations from one to the next, so without any particles sound cannot travel through space.
questions.hint: What does sound need that space doesn't have?
questions.examMistake: Saying space is 'too cold' — the real reason is no particles.

questions.q: What does the eardrum do when sound reaches it?
questions.modelAnswer: The eardrum vibrates when sound waves hit it, and it passes these vibrations on to the tiny bones inside the ear.
questions.hint: It moves when sound hits it.
questions.examMistake: Saying the eardrum 'hears' — it just vibrates.

questions.q: Roughly what is the human hearing range?
questions.explanation: Humans hear from about 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz.
questions.hint: Tens to tens-of-thousands of Hz.

questions.q: An echo is…
options: Sound reflected off a hard surface
questions.hint: Sound bouncing back.

questions.q: On an oscilloscope, how does a loud sound look different from a quiet sound?
questions.modelAnswer: A loud sound shows a wave with a larger amplitude (taller wave), while a quiet sound shows a wave with a smaller amplitude (shorter wave). The pitch (how close together the waves are) is unchanged by loudness.
questions.hint: Loudness changes the height of the wave.
questions.examMistake: Saying loud sounds look closer together — that's pitch, not loudness.

questions.q: Why do you see lightning before you hear thunder?
options: Light travels much faster than sound
questions.explanation: Light travels almost instantly; sound is much slower, so it arrives later.
questions.hint: Compare the speeds of light and sound.

questions.q: Explain how the sound from a drum reaches your ear.
questions.modelAnswer: When the drum is hit, the drum skin vibrates. This makes the air particles next to it vibrate, and they pass the vibration on to the next particles, forming a sound wave that travels through the air. When the wave reaches your ear it makes your eardrum vibrate, and these vibrations are turned into nerve signals that your brain understands as sound.
questions.hint: Trace it: drum vibrates → air → ear.
questions.examMistake: Saying the air 'carries' the sound without mentioning particles vibrating and passing it on.

questions.q: Two notes are played. Note A has a higher frequency than note B. Note A is…
questions.hint: Frequency → pitch.

questions.q: Explain why sound travels faster in water than in air.
questions.modelAnswer: In water the particles are much closer together than in air. Because the particles are closer, the vibrations are passed from one particle to the next more quickly, so the sound wave travels faster in water than in the more spread-out air.
questions.hint: Compare how close the particles are.
questions.examMistake: Saying water is 'thicker' without explaining particle spacing.

questions.q: On an oscilloscope, a high-pitched note shows…
questions.explanation: High pitch = high frequency = waves bunched closer together.
questions.hint: Pitch changes how close the waves are.

questions.q: A bell is rung inside a glass jar. As the air is pumped out, the sound fades to nothing. Explain why.
questions.modelAnswer: As the air is pumped out, there are fewer and fewer air particles to carry the sound vibrations from the bell to the jar. With almost no particles left (close to a vacuum), there is nothing to pass the vibrations on, so the sound cannot travel and fades to nothing, even though you can still see the bell moving.
questions.hint: What happens to the particles that carry sound?
questions.examMistake: Saying the bell stops vibrating — it still vibrates; there's just nothing to carry the sound.

questions.q: Which change would make a guitar string produce a higher-pitched note?
questions.explanation: Higher frequency (faster vibration) raises the pitch; plucking harder only changes loudness.
questions.hint: Pitch depends on frequency, not how hard you pluck.

questions.q: Describe the difference between how loudness and pitch are changed, in terms of the wave.
questions.modelAnswer: Loudness depends on the amplitude of the wave: a bigger vibration gives a larger amplitude and a louder sound. Pitch depends on the frequency of the wave: more vibrations per second gives a higher frequency and a higher pitch. They are independent, so a sound can be loud and low-pitched, or quiet and high-pitched.
questions.hint: One is about wave height, the other about how often it vibrates.
questions.examMistake: Treating loudness and pitch as if changing one changes the other.

questions.q: Ultrasound is sound that is…
questions.explanation: Ultrasound has a frequency above the human hearing range (20,000 Hz).
questions.hint: 'Ultra' = beyond the top of our range.

questions.q: A person stands 165 m from a cliff and shouts. They hear the echo 1 second later. Show how this fits the speed of sound being about 330 m/s.
questions.modelAnswer: The sound has to travel to the cliff and back, which is 165 m + 165 m = 330 m. It does this in 1 second. Speed = distance ÷ time = 330 ÷ 1 = 330 m/s, which matches the speed of sound in air.
questions.hint: Don't forget the sound travels there AND back.
questions.examMistake: Using only 165 m instead of the full there-and-back distance.

questions.q: Why might an older person not hear a very high-pitched whistle that a child can hear?
options: We lose the ability to hear high frequencies as we age
questions.explanation: Hearing of high frequencies declines with age, so the top of the range is lost.
questions.hint: Which end of the hearing range fades with age?

questions.q: Explain fully why sound is described as a longitudinal wave, using the idea of compressions and rarefactions.
questions.modelAnswer: In a sound wave the particles of the medium vibrate back and forth in the same direction that the wave travels — this is what makes it a longitudinal wave (unlike a transverse wave, where particles move at right angles to the wave). As the source vibrates, it pushes particles together to form regions of high pressure called compressions, and pulls them apart to form regions of low pressure called rarefactions. These compressions and rarefactions move outwards through the medium, carrying the energy of the sound.
questions.hint: Particle motion direction + the squashed/spread regions.
questions.examMistake: Describing particles moving at right angles — that's transverse, not sound.

questions.q: A ship uses sonar: it sends a sound pulse down and receives the echo from the seabed 0.4 s later. Sound travels at 1500 m/s in water. Calculate the depth and explain your method.
questions.modelAnswer: The total distance the sound travels is speed × time = 1500 × 0.4 = 600 m. But this is the distance down to the seabed and back up again, so the depth is half of this: 600 ÷ 2 = 300 m. The seabed is 300 m below the ship.
questions.hint: Find total distance, then halve it for the depth.
questions.examMistake: Forgetting to halve — the pulse goes down and back.

questions.q: Two oscilloscope traces show the same note from two instruments. Trace X has taller waves than trace Y, but the waves are equally spaced. What can you conclude?
options: X is higher pitched than Y
X is louder than Y but the same pitch
questions.explanation: Same spacing = same frequency = same pitch; taller waves = larger amplitude = louder. So X is louder at the same pitch.
questions.hint: Equal spacing means equal pitch; height means loudness.

questions.q: Explain why workers using loud machinery should wear ear defenders, referring to how we hear.
questions.modelAnswer: Very loud sounds have a large amplitude, meaning the air vibrates strongly. These strong vibrations make the eardrum and the delicate structures of the inner ear (such as the cochlea) vibrate very forcefully, which over time can damage them and cause permanent hearing loss. Ear defenders reduce the amplitude of the sound reaching the ear, protecting these structures.
questions.hint: Loud = large amplitude — what does that do to the ear?
questions.examMistake: Not linking loudness (amplitude) to physical damage of ear structures.

questions.q: Sound travels at about 340 m/s and light at 300,000,000 m/s. Explain how you could estimate how far away a thunderstorm is, and why the method works.
questions.modelAnswer: You count the seconds between seeing the lightning flash and hearing the thunder. Because light travels almost instantly over these distances, you effectively see the lightning at the moment it happens. The sound then takes time to reach you at about 340 m/s, so the gap in seconds tells you the travel time of the sound. Multiplying the time by 340 (or roughly, every 3 seconds ≈ 1 km) gives the distance to the storm.
questions.hint: The delay is essentially the sound's travel time.
questions.examMistake: Forgetting that the delay exists because sound is the slow one.

questions.q: Why does a sound become quieter the further you are from the source, even with no obstacles?
options: The wave's energy spreads over a larger area, so the amplitude reaching you is smaller
questions.explanation: Energy spreads out as the wave travels, reducing amplitude (loudness) with distance.
questions.hint: Think about the energy spreading out.

questions.q: Design an experiment to test how the speed of sound compares in air and along a solid such as a metal railing.
questions.modelAnswer: Independent variable: the medium (air vs the metal railing). Dependent variable: the time for a sound to travel a fixed distance. Method: have one person tap the end of a long railing while two listeners a measured distance away listen — one with an ear to the rail and one to the air. Measure (or compare) the arrival times. Control variables: the same distance, the same tap, the same temperature. The sound through the metal arrives first, showing sound travels faster in the solid because its particles are closer together. Repeat for reliability.
questions.hint: Compare arrival times over the same distance in two media.
questions.examMistake: Not keeping the distance the same, so the comparison is unfair.

questions.q: A guitarist makes a note higher by pressing the string against a fret to shorten it. Explain in terms of frequency why the note gets higher.
questions.modelAnswer: Shortening the vibrating length of the string makes it vibrate more quickly — it completes more vibrations each second, so the frequency increases. Because pitch depends on frequency, a higher frequency produces a higher-pitched note. (A longer, thicker or looser string vibrates more slowly, giving a lower pitch.)
questions.hint: Shorter string → faster vibration → ?
questions.examMistake: Saying it changes the loudness — shortening the string changes pitch.
